title: Damper
subtitle: Australian no-yeast campfire bread baked in the oven
excerpt: Self-raising flour, salt, and water or milk mixed into a rough dough and baked as a large round. No yeast, no proving, the Australian stockman's bread originally buried in campfire coals, adapted for a domestic oven.
sourceNotes: Damper is a traditional Australian bread associated with drovers and stockmen in the colonial period. The earliest documented descriptions appear in accounts of the Australian interior from the 1820s and 1830s. The method is consistent across all historical sources: flour, salt, and water worked quickly into a rough dough and baked in the coals of a campfire (or buried in ash). The domestic oven version is documented in Australian cookery guides from the late nineteenth century onward.

content.text: Damper is not quite a soda bread. Traditional damper used no leavener at all. It rose from the air mixed in by hand and the steam from the water in the dough. Modern versions use self-raising flour (which has baking powder in it) to get a more open crumb from a home oven. The key character of damper is how simple it is: few ingredients, light handling of the dough, straight to the heat.

Do not overwork the dough. Mix just until the flour is incorporated and the dough holds together, then stop. Overworking develops gluten and makes the damper tough and dense rather than crumbly and open.

attrs.prepNote: cold, cut into pieces (for a richer damper)
or water for the traditional version

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Put the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{fine-salt}} in a large bowl. If using, rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the flour until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Make a well and add the {{whole-milk}} or water. Stir with a fork until the dough just comes together. It will be rough and shaggy. Turn onto a lightly floured surface and press, do not knead, into a round about 20 cm across and 4 cm thick.

Transfer the round to the lined tray. Score a deep cross in the top with a sharp knife, cutting almost to the base: this helps the damper cook through evenly and allows it to be broken into four segments. Dust the top with a little flour. Bake for 25-30 minutes until the top is golden and the loaf sounds hollow when you tap the base. The internal temperature should read 90-92°C. Leave to cool for 10 minutes on the tray before breaking apart. Serve warm with butter.
